executive_summary:
This Health Facility Community Scorecard exercise engaged 180 community members across three locations in Zambia — Lusaka, Kitwe, and Ndola — with full response completion, as part of a participatory social accountability process assessing health facility performance from the community perspective.

Community sentiment toward facility governance and responsiveness is favourable overall (58.9% positive, 27.2% neutral, 13.9% negative). Consistent with the participatory scorecard methodology's purpose — surfacing specific, actionable accountability gaps rather than a single satisfaction number — three distinct scoring themes emerged with different weight: service quality concerns were most frequently raised (7 mentions), followed by affordability (5) and access (3).

Within the service-quality theme, community members specifically credited facility-level complaint mechanisms as a welcome improvement, while simultaneously and independently noting that facility budget and resource allocation information is still not shared with the community — a specific transparency gap the scorecard process is well-suited to formally document and carry into the next facility-community dialogue session.

The sample achieved a favourable gender balance skewing toward female participation (52.8% female, 47.2% male) — a meaningful signal for health facility scorecards specifically, given women's typically higher direct interaction with maternal and child health services. Community engagement was strongest in Lusaka, with all three locations meeting adequate participation thresholds for a valid scorecard exercise.
key_findings: Service quality is the most-scored theme (7 mentions), ahead of affordability (5) and access (3) — this scorecard prioritization should directly inform the agenda of the next facility-community dialogue meeting.

Facility-level complaint mechanisms are specifically credited as a welcome improvement — a concrete, scorecard-documented governance win for the facility to carry forward into its own performance record.

Facility budget and resource allocation transparency is independently and repeatedly flagged as unmet — a specific accountability gap the community explicitly wants addressed, distinct from the service-quality praise given elsewhere.

Community sentiment is favourable in all three locations, with Ndola showing the smallest participant base — future scorecard rounds should specifically verify Ndola facility engagement is not under-represented relative to its actual catchment population.

Female participation (52.8%) exceeds male participation in this round, which is a meaningful and appropriate signal for health facility scorecards given typically higher female engagement with maternal and child health services specifically.

All three locations met adequate participation thresholds for a valid community scorecard exercise, supporting confidence in using these results for the next facility-community accountability dialogue.
discussion:
The community scorecard methodology is specifically designed to produce a structured, facility-specific accountability record that both the community and facility management can jointly review and act on — and this round's finding pattern (specific praise for complaint mechanisms, specific concern about budget transparency) is exactly the kind of dual-sided, actionable output the methodology is intended to generate, rather than a single blended satisfaction score.

The budget transparency gap identified here should be treated as a standing agenda item for the next facility-community interface meeting specifically because the scorecard process creates a formal, documented, joint-review mechanism for tracking whether it is addressed — distinguishing this from a general citizen survey where such a finding might not have a clear, built-in follow-up structure.
conclusions: This scorecard round documents genuine community recognition of improved facility complaint mechanisms, alongside a specific, unresolved transparency gap regarding facility budget and resource allocation. Both findings should be carried into the next facility-community dialogue session as the two standing agenda items, consistent with the scorecard methodology's joint accountability purpose.

immediate: Present this scorecard's findings at the next facility-community interface meeting, with the complaint-mechanism improvement and budget transparency gap as the two named agenda items.
Facility management should prepare a specific response to the budget transparency finding ahead of that meeting, rather than allowing it to be raised without a prepared institutional response.
medium_term: Establish a simple, standing facility budget summary display (physical noticeboard or verbal briefing at the community interface meeting) as the facility's direct response to this scorecard's transparency finding.
Verify Ndola facility community engagement levels ahead of the next scorecard round, given its comparatively smaller participant base in this round relative to Lusaka and Kitwe.
long_term: Institutionalize the community scorecard as a recurring, scheduled accountability mechanism (e.g., biannual) rather than a one-off exercise, so the budget transparency gap identified here can be formally tracked to resolution across multiple cycles.

Use this round's gender participation pattern (52.8% female) as a baseline for verifying future rounds maintain meaningful engagement from both the population most directly served by maternal/child health services and the broader community.

risks: If the budget transparency finding is not carried forward into a documented facility-community dialogue outcome, the scorecard process risks being perceived as consultative without being genuinely accountability-generating.
Lower relative participation in Ndola, if not addressed, could result in future scorecard rounds under-representing that location's community perspective relative to its actual population.
opportunities: The complaint-mechanism improvement is a concrete, scorecard-documented success the facility can formally record and reference in its own performance reporting to district health management.

Because the transparency gap was raised consistently rather than by an isolated respondent, a single, low-cost facility-level transparency measure (a budget noticeboard) could address it directly at minimal institutional cost.
lessons_learned: This round confirms the value of the community scorecard's structured, dual-sided format (crediting specific improvements while separately documenting specific unresolved gaps) over a single blended satisfaction rating — it produced a clear, two-item agenda for the next facility-community dialogue rather than a general impression that would be harder to act on directly. Future scorecard rounds should preserve this structured format and continue tracking both dimensions as distinct, named items.
